section.title: My Help Comes from the Lord
section.sectionRationale: The second of the fifteen Songs of Ascents, Psalms 120 to 134, sung by pilgrims going up to Jerusalem, placed in Book V of the Psalter, Psalms 107 to 150. It answers the distress of Psalm 120 with a confident confession of the LORD as the pilgrim's tireless guardian on the road.
section.thesis: The LORD who made heaven and earth is an ever watchful keeper who never slumbers, sheltering His people from every danger by day and night, from this moment until forevermore.
section.frameNote: Two movements marked by a question and its answer: the question of where help comes from and the confession that it comes from the LORD, verses 1 to 2, and the sixfold declaration of His unsleeping keeping, verses 3 to 8.

units.label: My help comes from the Lord

verses.text: I lift up my eyes to the hills. From where does my help come?
My help comes from the LORD, the Maker of heaven and earth.

units.label: He who keeps you will not slumber

verses.text: He will not allow your foot to slip; your {{shomer|Protector}} will not slumber.
Behold, the Protector of Israel will neither slumber nor sleep.
The LORD is your keeper; the LORD is the shade on your right hand.
The sun will not strike you by day, nor the moon by night.
The LORD will guard you from all evil; He will preserve your {{nephesh|soul}}.
The LORD will watch over your coming and going, both now and forevermore.

shomer.gloss: Keeper, guardian, watchman. The same root, shamar, is repeated six times across this short psalm, forming its backbone: the LORD is the tireless watchman who never dozes off duty.

nephesh.gloss: Soul, life, the whole self. The LORD's keeping reaches beyond the physical journey to preserve the pilgrim's very life and being.

basic.context: This Song of Ascents pictures a pilgrim scanning the hills along the road to Jerusalem, hills that could hide bandits as easily as offer help, and answering his own question: help comes not from the hills but from the LORD who made them.
basic.hermeneutics: It describes the specific comfort of pilgrims facing real dangers on the road, sun, moon, slipping feet, and it prescribes as an abiding pattern trusting the LORD's unsleeping care rather than the created world itself.
basic.originalLanguages: The Hebrew root shamar, keep or guard, is repeated six times, forming a drumbeat of assurance. The LORD's keeping reaches to the nephesh, the whole soul and life, not merely the body on the road.
basic.typology: The LORD's tireless watch, who neither slumbers nor sleeps, anticipates Christ, who though He slept in the boat in His humanity, awoke to command the wind and waves with the same divine authority this psalm ascribes to God alone.
basic.christ: Jesus is the good shepherd who never slumbers over His sheep, John 10:28 to 29, and He is Immanuel, God with us, whose keeping power is not confined to a single road or a single lifetime but reaches to eternal life.
basic.god: God is the Maker of heaven and earth who personally keeps His people, unsleeping, unshaded from His care by day or by night, guarding both body and soul from this moment forever.
basic.crossReferences: John 10:28-29 shows Jesus keeping His sheep so that none can be snatched away. Mark 4:38-39 shows Jesus, though asleep in the boat, awaking with full authority over the storm. Jude 24-25 praises the One able to keep believers from stumbling.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's confession that help comes only from the Maker of heaven and earth runs forward to Christ, in whom that same unsleeping, all sufficient keeping is given fully and finally to His people.

context.literary: A Song of Ascents structured as question and answer, verses 1 to 2, followed by a sixfold repetition of the LORD's keeping, verses 3 to 8, using the same Hebrew root, shamar, throughout.
context.historical: The road from Galilee or the Jordan Valley up to Jerusalem climbed steep, exposed terrain where bandits, heat, and cold were real dangers, making this psalm's picture of tireless divine protection immediately practical for pilgrims.
context.canonical: Follows directly after Psalm 120's cry from distress, answering that opening lament with the confident assurance that the LORD who hears also keeps His people every step of the way.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure pilgrims, and all who trust the LORD, that His care is not occasional but constant, covering the whole journey of life from beginning to end.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes the specific comfort offered to pilgrims traveling a dangerous road. It prescribes as an abiding pattern trusting the LORD, not the created order itself, as the true and only source of help.

debates.question: Does lifting the eyes to the hills in verse 1 express hope or apprehension?

views.label: The hills represent hope, since Jerusalem and the temple sit on high ground
views.case: Pilgrims literally looked toward the hills of Zion as their destination and source of worship, so the gaze is naturally hopeful.

views.label: The hills represent danger, since bandits and pagan high places lurked there
views.case: The question that follows, 'From where does my help come', reads more naturally as genuine uncertainty answered by turning away from the hills themselves to the LORD who made them.

debates.bearing: Either reading reaches the same conclusion: the psalmist's confidence rests not in the hills as such but in the LORD who made heaven and earth.

study.originalLanguages: The Hebrew root shamar, to keep or guard, repeats six times, forming the psalm's steady rhythm of assurance. The LORD's keeping reaches the nephesh, the whole soul and life, not merely the body braving the road.

typology.type: The LORD who neither slumbers nor sleeps, verses 3-4
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who though He slept in His humanity, Mark 4:38, awoke to command the storm with the same divine authority this psalm gives to God alone, Mark 4:39.

typology.type: The LORD as shade and shelter, verse 5
typology.fulfillment: Christ as the good shepherd who keeps His sheep so that none is snatched from His hand, John 10:28-29.

study.christ: Jesus is the good shepherd whose keeping never slumbers, John 10:28-29, the fulfillment of this psalm's confession that Israel's Protector neither slumbers nor sleeps. In His humanity He did sleep, Mark 4:38, yet He awoke to still the storm with the very authority the psalm attributes to the LORD alone, Mark 4:39, showing Him to be the help that comes from the Maker of heaven and earth.
study.god: God is the Maker of heaven and earth who personally, tirelessly keeps His people, unshaded from His care by sun or moon, guarding body and soul from this time forth and forevermore.

crossReferences.note: Jesus keeps His sheep so that no one can snatch them from His hand or the Father's, echoing this psalm's unsleeping Protector.
Jesus, asleep in the boat yet awaking to command the storm, displays the same divine authority this psalm ascribes to the LORD who never slumbers.
Praises the One able to keep believers from stumbling, the New Testament's echo of this psalm's confidence in God's keeping power.

study.oneStory: This psalm's confession that help comes only from the Maker of heaven and earth runs forward to Christ, in whom that same unsleeping, all sufficient keeping is given fully and finally to His people, now and forevermore.

sermon.title: My Help Comes from the Lord
sermon.why: Begg preaches this psalm directly by name, walking through its picture of the LORD as the pilgrim's tireless keeper and drawing out the assurance this gives for facing daily dangers with confidence in God's unsleeping care.

quiz.q: Where does the psalmist say his help ultimately comes from?

options.text: From the LORD, the Maker of heaven and earth.
options.note: Verse 2 gives this direct answer to the question of verse 1.

options.text: From the hills themselves.
options.note: The hills prompt the question, but the answer redirects to the LORD who made them.

options.text: From his own strength and preparation.
options.note: The psalm explicitly locates help outside the psalmist, in the LORD.

options.text: From the watchmen on the city walls.
options.note: Watchmen appear elsewhere, such as Psalm 130, but here help comes from the LORD directly.

quiz.q: What is repeated to describe the LORD's care throughout this psalm?

options.text: That He keeps or guards His people, using the Hebrew root shamar six times.
options.note: This repetition forms the psalm's structural backbone.

options.text: That He forgives sin repeatedly.
options.note: Forgiveness is the theme of Psalm 130, not the keeping language here.

options.text: That He rebuilds the temple.
options.note: Temple building is not this psalm's subject.

options.text: That He blesses the harvest.
options.note: Harvest blessing appears in psalms like 126 and 128, not centrally here.

quiz.q: What does verse 4 emphasize about the LORD's watchfulness?

options.text: He will neither slumber nor sleep.
options.note: This is stated directly, intensifying verse 3's claim.

options.text: He rests on the Sabbath from watching.
options.note: The text specifically denies any slumber or sleep, even for rest.

options.text: He watches only during the day.
options.note: Verse 6 specifically extends His care to nighttime as well.

options.text: He watches only over kings.
options.note: The psalm says He watches over Israel broadly, not kings alone.

quiz.q: How far does the LORD's promised keeping extend, according to verse 8?

options.text: Over every coming and going, from this time forth and forevermore.
options.note: Verse 8 gives this comprehensive, eternal scope.

options.text: Only for the duration of one pilgrimage.
options.note: The psalm's language is deliberately unlimited, not tied to a single journey.

options.text: Only until the pilgrim reaches Jerusalem.
options.note: The promise is not bounded by arrival at a destination.

options.text: Only during the pilgrim's youth.
options.note: The text specifies forevermore, not a limited season of life.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's unsleeping Protector connect to Christ in the Gospels?

options.text: Jesus, though asleep in the boat, awoke to command the storm with the same divine authority this psalm gives to God alone.
options.note: Mark 4:38-39 shows Jesus exercising this very authority over creation.

options.text: It has no connection to the New Testament.
options.note: The Gospels directly echo this psalm's language of divine authority over storms and keeping.

options.text: It shows Jesus needing rescue from danger Himself.
options.note: The Gospel account shows Jesus as the one who calms danger, not one needing rescue.

options.text: It predicts the exact number of Jesus' miracles.
options.note: The connection is one of shared divine authority and pattern, not a numerical prediction.

questions: The psalmist lifts his eyes to the hills and then answers his own question by turning to the LORD. Where are you tempted to look for help in places that cannot ultimately give it?
Six times this psalm says the LORD keeps His people. Which specific situation in your life right now most needs to be entrusted to His unsleeping care?
The LORD is described as a shade against the sun and a guard against the moon. What fears, day or night, do you need to bring to Him as your shelter?
Jesus, though asleep in the boat, awoke with full authority over the storm. How does knowing Christ holds this same unsleeping power change how you face today's uncertainty?
The promise reaches from now to forevermore. How does trusting God's care for your whole life, not just this season, change the way you pray about your future?

translationNotes.note: The question 'From where does my help come' can be read as either hopeful anticipation of Zion or genuine uncertainty about danger in the hills; either way the answer that follows locates help in the LORD alone.
Protector renders shomer, keeper or guardian, from the root shamar which recurs six times across the psalm and forms its central theme.
Ancient belief associated both sunstroke and a form of moon related affliction with travel; the psalm answers both fears with the LORD's comprehensive, round the clock protection.

meta.notes: BSB text taken verbatim from biblehub.com's BSB page for Psalm 121 (the local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error, so the web fallback was used for all of Psalms 120-132). Verse count (8) and wording cross-checked against the section map bounds Psalms 121:1-8. No em or en dashes present. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming Alistair Begg's dedicated sermon 'My Help Comes from the Lord' at truthforlife.org.
